texto: A fan is like the thighs of a woman: It opens and closes. A good fan opens with a flick of the wrist. It produces its own weather---a breeze not so strong as to muss the hair.
explicacao: Metáfora atribuída a Rikki Ducornet: compara elementos do cotidiano para falar de temas ligados a reflexao. O trecho “A fan is like the thighs of a woman: It opens and closes. A good fan opens with a flick of the wr…” usa imagem concreta para lembrar que reflexao, erotica, sensuous pedem movimento e interpretação pessoal, em tom de sensuous.

texto: I, sole heir to the Munodi line and memory, am childless. A friend who knows such things has told me that this explains my compulsion to capture what I can with black ink on white paper." ("The Volatilized Ceiling of Baron Munodi")
explicacao: Citação de Rikki Ducornet sobre temas ligados a amizade. “I, sole heir to the Munodi line and memory, am childless. A friend who knows such things has told…” articula amizade, children, creativity em registro de amizade, com relevância para contextos como termino e creativity.

texto: What are books but tangible dreams? What is reading if it is not dreaming? The best books cause us to dream the rest are not worth reading.
explicacao: Pergunta retórica de Rikki Ducornet que provoca temas ligados a motivacao. Ao questionar “What are books but tangible dreams? What is reading if it is not dreaming? The best books cause u…”, o texto convida o leitor a revisar crenças sobre motivacao, books, reading, com foco em reading.

texto: A book is a private thing, citizen; it belongs to the one who writes it and to the one who reads it. Like the mind itself, a book is a private space. Within that space, anything is possible. The greatest evil and the greatest good.
explicacao: Metáfora atribuída a Rikki Ducornet: compara elementos do cotidiano para falar de temas ligados a reflexao. O trecho “A book is a private thing, citizen; it belongs to the one who writes it and to the one who reads …” usa imagem concreta para lembrar que reflexao, books, censorship pedem movimento e interpretação pessoal, em tom de censorship e de-sade.

texto: Next I prayed to Allah, whose ears are deaf; then did I beseech his fallen twin, the Devil Hornprick, who sits upon his thorn of fire, gloating upon his constellations and counting his bloody seeds. In Baclava it is said Hornprick once caught a glimpse of the First Woman, as she sat singing to her snake in her chamber of sacred mud. Dazzled by her sight, the light of love and lust, he fell. He is still falling. For all eternity her breasts orbit his dreams.
explicacao: Citação de Rikki Ducornet sobre vínculos afetivos e cuidado mútuo. “Next I prayed to Allah, whose ears are deaf; then did I beseech his fallen twin, the Devil Hornpr…” articula motivacao, damnation, devil em registro de motivacao, com relevância para contextos como devil e woman.
